gm.name: Brad Holmes
gm.title: Executive Vice President and General Manager
gm.previous_role: Director of College Scouting, Los Angeles Rams

career_path.mentor: Wilbert Montgomery (running backs coach, facilitated transition to scouting)

career_path.role: Executive Vice President and General Manager
career_path.mentor: Self (with advisory influences from John Dorsey, Ray Agnew)

scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Snead/Rams tree. Holmes worked under three Rams GMs (Charley Armey, Billy Devaney, Les Snead) but his formative years as Director of College Scouting (2013-2020) were entirely under Snead. The Rams philosophy under Snead emphasized willingness to trade aggressively for elite talent, scheme-versatile players, and eventually pivoted to trading draft capital for proven stars. Holmes absorbed the build-through-the-draft foundation but has explicitly acknowledged he may eventually adopt Snead's 'F them picks' approach as the roster matures. Also trained through National Football Scouting (the organization that runs the Combine), giving him rigorous traditional scouting methodology. Jerry Reese (former Giants GM) served as a personal role model.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Holmes diverges from Snead in three key areas: (1) He places dramatically higher emphasis on 'football character' — intangibles like passion, work ethic, leadership, and accountability are non-negotiable, not just tiebreakers. This was shaped by his experience scouting Aaron Donald and Cooper Kupp. (2) He explicitly does NOT keep a depth chart in the draft room, unlike past Rams regimes he worked under, to avoid being swayed by needs. (3) He maintains pre-draft top-30 visits as essential even as Snead has abandoned them, valuing the human evaluation component.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 5 drafts of data as Lions GM. Lineage still informs general philosophy but Holmes has established a distinct, well-documented behavioral profile of his own. Lineage weight is low because we have extensive primary evidence.

key_influencers.name: Dan Campbell
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Physical, tough, culture-first players. Values toughness and grit above all. Offensive line background. Campbell and Holmes are described as deeply aligned and collaborative; Holmes has repeatedly said 'as long as Dan and I are aligned and convicted' on a player, they take him. Campbell specifically endorsed Jameson Williams after seeing him make a special teams tackle as a gunner — football character manifested.

key_influencers.name: Ray Agnew
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Rams colleague of Holmes (Director of Pro Scouting with Rams for 4 years, 18 total years with Rams scouting staff after playing career). Shares the Snead/Rams philosophical DNA. Involved in free agency evaluation and roster construction strategy. Holmes credits Agnew as a key partner in the Rams-to-Lions philosophical transition.

key_influencers.name: John Dorsey
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Director
key_influencers.known_biases: Former GM of Chiefs (drafted Patrick Mahomes) and Browns (drafted Baker Mayfield). Known for aggressive talent evaluation and willingness to take big swings. Credited by Holmes with identifying Giovanni Manu as a prospect. Brings an experienced voice and different scouting perspective to the room.

front_office.power_dynamics: Highly collaborative but Holmes has final say. Holmes and Campbell operate as genuine partners with deep alignment on culture and player evaluation. Holmes described it as making sure they are 'aligned and convicted' on picks. The front office structure is described as bringing a large group together to work effectively. Holmes credits his scouts as 'the experts on all these players' and trusts their evaluations. Campbell has significant input on offensive personnel and culture fit, but Holmes holds the pen.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Best player available with emphasis on football character. Does not keep a depth chart in the draft room. 'We don't really get anchored on positions. We don't really get anchored on windows.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Genuinely BPA-leaning, especially in Round 1, but with a clear filter: 'football character' (work ethic, passion, leadership, accountability, mental toughness) is a non-negotiable prerequisite. Holmes has said 'intangibles are the separators of success' and 'football character is probably the biggest thing.' He has taken perceived reaches (Gibbs at 12, Campbell at 18, back-to-back DTs in 2021) because those players were highest on HIS board regardless of consensus. However, he is willing to address clear roster weaknesses — in 2024 he took two CBs with his first two picks when it was the team's biggest need, and in 2022 he drafted six defenders out of eight picks when the defense was terrible.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Minimal mismatch. Holmes practices what he preaches more than most GMs. He genuinely drafts off his own board, which regularly diverges from consensus. The 2023 draft (only 14 first-round grades, Campbell at 18 was not 14th on that list) is the clearest evidence of a GM who truly trusts his own evaluation over public boards. When asked about need in 2024, Holmes said 'with where the roster is now, I think you even have more flexibility to not be anchored into a need.'

trade_triggers: Strong conviction on a specific player ('if there's a player that we have buy-in and we want, I'm going to try to get that player')
Tier break on the board where a coveted player is in danger of being taken
Accumulating additional Day 2/3 picks when confident in depth of class at positions of interest
Strategic move to acquire future draft capital (traded down from 6 to 12 in 2023)
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Build-through-the-draft first, but increasingly willing to trade picks for proven players as roster matures. Holmes explicitly acknowledged the Snead/Rams progression from draft-building to trading picks for stars, saying 'we're getting closer' to that approach. Has traded future picks for players (3rd-round pick for Carlton Davis, 5th for Za'Darius Smith). Still values having multiple Day 2/3 selections for developmental depth. Traded up 20 spots in Round 1 for Jameson Williams (2022), traded up from 29 to 24 for Terrion Arnold (2024), traded up in Rounds 2-3 multiple times in 2023 and 2025.

position_avoidance: Has not drafted an IOL in Rounds 1-3 until 2025 (Ratledge, Round 2). Generally addresses IOL later or in free agency. Has not drafted a QB in Rounds 1-2.
position_premium: DT — drafted DTs in Rounds 1-3 in four of five drafts (Onwuzurike R2 2021, McNeill R3 2021, Brodric Martin R3 2023, Tyleik Williams R1 2025). EDGE is consistently addressed early. CB received heavy investment when it was finally needed (two in top-61 in 2024).

EDGE.archetype_summary: Productive, high-motor, versatile defensive end with elite football character and positional flexibility. Holmes wants EDGE players who can line up in multiple spots along the defensive front — standing up and hand in dirt, inside on sub-packages and outside on early downs. Production and effort trump pure physical traits. Think polished, high-floor rushers with relentless motors, not raw athletic freaks.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard minimum — drafted Hutchinson despite 32 1/8 inch arms that were considered short. Holmes explicitly weighed whether short arms were disqualifying (like he did with Donald's size) and decided production overruled measurables.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness and closing burst matter more than raw 40 time. Hutchinson ran 4.74 but his film speed and 6.73 three-cone were elite. Vertical jump (36 inches for Hutchinson) valued as explosiveness indicator.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff. Paschal was not a speed rusher. Values play speed over timed speed.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Polished hand usage, motor, and effort — wins with technique and relentlessness rather than a single elite physical trait
most_valued_traits: Relentless motor and effort consistency
Positional versatility (can line up inside and outside)
College production — must have demonstrated ability to get to the QB
Leadership and football character (Hutchinson: Heisman finalist leader; Paschal: 3-time captain, cancer survivor; Houston: dominant production)
Technical hand usage and pass-rush repertoire
least_important_traits: Elite bend/flexibility — Hutchinson 'doesn't have elite bend at the top of his rush' per Jeremiah but was still taken 2nd overall
Raw measurables like 40 time or arm length when production is elite
deal_breakers: Lack of effort or motor on film
Character concerns or history of taking plays off
No demonstrated pass-rush production in college
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite college production combined with leadership/character. Holmes ran up the card on Hutchinson (14 sacks, Heisman finalist, team captain) and didn't hesitate at pick 2.

college_production.production_floor: Hutchinson had 14 sacks in his final year; Paschal had 5.5 sacks in final year; Houston had 16.5 sacks at Jackson State; Hassanein had 9.5 sacks in final year. Holmes wants demonstrated pass-rush production.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production and breakout seasons — Hutchinson's 14-sack senior year after injury-shortened junior year was the defining data point.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — valued Houston's domination of FCS competition, valued Paschal's production in the SEC, adjusted for level of play.

examples: 2022 R1.2 — Aidan Hutchinson: 6-7, 260, 4.74 40, 32 1/8 arms, 36-inch vert. 14 sacks at Michigan, Heisman finalist. Polished, relentless motor, high-character leader.
2022 R2.46 — Josh Paschal: 6-3, 268. Versatile DE who played edge and inside at Kentucky. 3-time captain, cancer survivor. Football character exemplar.
2022 R6.217 — James Houston: 6-2, 248. Transfer from Florida to Jackson State under Deion Sanders. 16.5 sacks in final season. Pass-rush specialist with insane production.
2025 R6.196 — Ahmed Hassanein: 6-2, 250. Boise State edge. 9.5 sacks in final season. First player from Egypt to play FBS football. Holmes: 'plays with his hair on fire.'

OT.archetype_summary: Elite athletic offensive tackle with massive upside, scheme versatility, and physical dominance. Holmes is willing to invest premium draft capital in franchise-caliber tackles with rare athletic profiles, even if raw. Values positional versatility and long-term development potential. Penei Sewell is the archetype — generational athlete with size, power, and movement skills.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches for tackles (Manu: 34 inch arms; Sewell: 33+ inch arms). Holmes clearly values length at OT, citing analytics studies on arm length and OT success rates.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Burst score and explosiveness matter most. Manu had the #1 athleticism score in the entire 2024 OL class. Sewell was considered a generational athlete. 40 time secondary to movement skills and power.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff but wants athletic tackles who can move in space. Manu ran 5.12 (74th percentile for OL). Sewell was elite mover.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Physical dominance at the point of attack combined with elite movement skills for a zone-based scheme
most_valued_traits: Elite athleticism relative to position — scheme requires tackles who can reach-block and move laterally
Size and length to mirror and redirect speed rushers
Toughness and physicality in the run game
Positional versatility (Sewell played LT in college, moved to RT; Manu may play guard; Sorsdal versatile)
Football character and coachability
least_important_traits: Polished technique at draft time — willing to develop raw prospects (Manu from Canadian college football)
College starting experience at a Power 5 school (Manu: UBC; Sorsdal: William & Mary)
deal_breakers: Lack of athletic upside or movement ability
Character or effort concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Rare size-athleticism combination. Holmes traded a future 3rd-round pick to move up for Manu (6-7, 352, elite athleticism from a Canadian school) — pure projection bet based on physical tools and character.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred (Sewell: 2 years at Oregon; Sorsdal: 46 career starts at W&M) but willing to take raw projects (Manu: Canadian college level).
college_production.production_trajectory: Values durability and toughness through the season more than statistical production metrics.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — took Manu from University of British Columbia, showing willingness to look beyond traditional Power 5 pipeline.

examples: 2021 R1.7 — Penei Sewell: 6-5, 331, Oregon. Generational OT prospect. 95.5 PFF grade. Holmes' first-ever pick as GM. Franchise cornerstone who moved from LT to RT.

2024 R4.126 — Giovanni Manu: 6-7, 352, 34-inch arms, 5.12 40. From University of British Columbia (first NFL draft pick from UBC). Pure projection/developmental pick based on rare size-athleticism combo.
2023 R5.152 — Colby Sorsdal: 6-6, 300, William & Mary. 46 career starts. Small-school OT with versatility. Team ranked 4th nationally in fewest sacks allowed.

IOL.archetype_summary: Tough, versatile interior linemen who can play multiple positions and fit the physical, run-first culture. Holmes has historically addressed IOL later in the draft or in free agency, but showed willingness to invest Day 2 capital in 2025. Values toughness, durability, positional versatility, and ability to play in a zone-based scheme. SEC and Power 5 pedigree preferred for the higher picks.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear minimum established from draft picks
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Values strength and anchor more than combine athleticism at this position. Mahogany was a late-round pick valued for pass protection dominance (1 sack allowed in 711 pass-blocking snaps over 3 seasons).
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard speed requirement. Zone scheme demands adequate movement skills but doesn't require elite athletic testing.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Toughness, durability, and ability to play through adversity — fits the Detroit culture
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility — Holmes specifically noted Ratledge and Frazier would be 'cross-trained at multiple positions'
Toughness and ability to play through injuries (Ratledge: 'played through some serious toughness, played through some injury stuff')
Pass protection reliability
Football character and locker room fit
Experience at a high level of competition
least_important_traits: Elite athletic testing numbers
Single-position specialization
deal_breakers: Inability to play multiple interior positions
Lack of toughness or durability concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Versatility combined with toughness and high-level experience. Holmes traded up for both Ratledge and Frazier in 2025.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter at a competitive program preferred. Ratledge: started 14+ games at Georgia for national championship teams. Mahogany: multi-year starter at Boston College.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers players who have started extensively and improved over time.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values conference competition level — SEC guard (Ratledge from Georgia) gets premium; ACC guard (Mahogany from BC) still valued for production.

examples: 2025 R2.57 — Tate Ratledge: 6-6, 318, Georgia. Multi-year starter at RG for national champions. Holmes valued his toughness, intelligence, versatility, and SEC experience.
2025 R5.171 — Miles Frazier: 6-4, 308, LSU. Holmes traded up because of high conviction. Noted as fitting culture 'like a glove.' Team captain wearing #18 at LSU (highest character honor).
2024 R6.210 — Christian Mahogany: 6-3, 320, Boston College. Fell further than expected. Allowed just 1 sack and 7 pressures from 711 pass-blocking snaps over 3 seasons.

DT.archetype_summary: Versatile, powerful interior defenders who can play multiple techniques and contribute in both run defense and pass rush. Holmes has drafted DTs more aggressively than almost any other position — 6 DTs in 5 drafts including a 1st-rounder. Values size, strength at the point of attack, and untapped pass-rush potential. Willing to invest in raw, athletic interior linemen and develop them. Think Aaron Donald pipeline — the DT who does more than expected.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear hard minimum. Has drafted across a range of sizes.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Values explosiveness and pass-rush potential. McNeill developed from a run-stuffer into a pass-rush threat. Tyleik Williams (334 lbs, 4.68 40) valued for rare size-athleticism combo.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff but wants interior linemen who can be disruptive as pass rushers, not just space-eaters.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Strength at the point of attack with untapped pass-rush upside — Holmes wants DTs who can develop into disruptors, not just hold gaps
most_valued_traits: Versatility to play multiple techniques along the interior (3-tech, 5-tech, nose)
Strength at the point of attack to control the line of scrimmage
Pass-rush potential — Holmes said about Williams: 'just the balance that he's able to offer in both phases'
Motor and effort
Football character and team leadership (Williams: Ohio State captain; Paschal: 3-time captain; Martin: raw but high-effort)
least_important_traits: Polished pass-rush move repertoire at draft time — willing to develop raw rushers
Combine measurable perfection — Williams had questions about arm length but production and athleticism overruled
deal_breakers: One-dimensional run-only defender with no pass-rush upside
Lack of effort or inconsistent motor
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Rare combination of size, explosiveness, and football character. Holmes took back-to-back DTs in 2021 (Onwuzurike R2, McNeill R3) when the team had many other needs, because they were the best players on his board.

college_production.production_floor: Varies widely — Onwuzurike had modest stats but elite film; Martin had 1.5 sacks but was a raw developmental pick; Williams had 5 sacks as a freshman but modest senior stats. Holmes evaluates film and potential over raw stats at DT.
college_production.production_trajectory: Willing to take players with ascending development curves or whose stats don't match their film impact.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusted for role, scheme, and rotation usage. Values Senior Bowl and workout performances (Martin got on radar at East-West Shrine Bowl; Williams impressed at Senior Bowl).

examples: 2025 R1.28 — Tyleik Williams: 6-3, 334, Ohio State. National champion, team captain. Combination of size, strength, athleticism. Holmes valued his ability to contribute in run and pass phases.
2021 R2.41 — Levi Onwuzurike: 6-3, 290, Washington. Penetrating 3-tech with quickness. First-team All-Pac-12. Opt-out year.
2021 R3.72 — Alim McNeill: 6-2, 320, NC State. Run-stopping nose who developed into pass-rush threat. Broke out in 2023 to Pro Bowl level.
2023 R3.96 — Brodric Martin: 6-5, 337, Western Kentucky. Raw developmental DT. Not invited to Combine. Got on radar at East-West Shrine Bowl.
2024 R5.189 — Mekhi Wingo: 6-1, 285, LSU. Team captain wearing #18 (LSU's highest character honor). Holmes saw edge-rush potential beyond interior role.

CB.archetype_summary: Physical, ball-hawking cornerbacks with size, football IQ, and versatility. Holmes prioritizes ball skills (interceptions, pass breakups), physicality in run support, and scheme versatility over pure speed. Willing to take corners who can play multiple spots in the secondary. The 2024 draft (two CBs in first two rounds) provided the clearest picture of Holmes' CB preferences.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31+ inches preferred (Arnold: 31.63 inches). Melifonwu at 6-3 was the long/tall prototype. Has shown range in size preferences.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Ball skills and football IQ matter more than raw 40 time. Arnold ran 4.50 (underwhelming for a CB) but Holmes traded UP for him. Melifonwu was a physical specimen (6-3, 205). Values vertical jump and burst.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff — Arnold's 4.50 was considered underwhelming by most teams but Holmes valued his film and ball skills enough to trade up. Not a 4.40 threshold GM.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Versatile — can play man and zone. Arnold played mostly outside but had slot experience. Branch was a hybrid safety/nickel. Rakestraw excelled in zone coverage (85.2 PFF zone grade).
most_valued_traits: Ball skills and turnover creation — Holmes said 'can he track and locate? Can he take the ball away?' about evaluating corners. Arnold had 5 INTs and 12 PBUs.
Physicality in run support — Arnold led SEC CBs with 90.6 PFF run-defense grade
Football IQ and scheme understanding — valued Arnold's 'smart and understanding of football'
Positional versatility — Branch played safety/slot hybrid; Arnold had inside/outside experience
Competitive toughness
least_important_traits: Elite timed speed — 4.50 was acceptable for the first CB taken
Pure press-man technique as a prerequisite — Rakestraw was primarily a zone corner
deal_breakers: Inability to find the football and create turnovers
Poor tackler or unwilling run defender
Character red flags
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite ball production combined with physicality. Holmes traded from 29 to 24 to get Arnold, who had 5 INTs and led the SEC in run-defense grade among CBs.

college_production.production_floor: Values turnover creation. Arnold: 5 INTs, 12 PBUs in final year. Branch: dominant tackling DB. Rakestraw: strong zone coverage grades. No hard statistical floor but wants evidence of playmaking.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending producers. Arnold improved dramatically from sophomore to junior year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values SEC and high-level competition. Arnold and Branch both from Alabama.

examples: 2024 R1.24 — Terrion Arnold: 6-0, 189, 4.50 40, 31.63 arms. Alabama. 5 INTs, 12 PBUs, 90.6 PFF run-defense grade. Traded up from 29 to 24.
2024 R2.61 — Ennis Rakestraw Jr: 6-0, 190, Missouri. Physical CB who excelled in zone coverage. 85.2 PFF zone grade. Holmes: 'we had ranked very, very high.'
2021 R3.101 — Ifeatu Melifonwu: 6-3, 205, Syracuse. Long, physical prototype. Seen as 2nd-round value in 3rd round. Converted safety/corner with rare length.
2023 R2.45 — Brian Branch: 6-0, 190, Alabama. Hybrid safety/nickel. 539 slot snaps at Alabama. Best tackling DB since PFF started tracking (2% missed tackle rate). A true do-it-all defender.
2022 R7.237 — Chase Lucas: 5-11, 185, Arizona State. 6-year senior, 49 career starts. Experience and special teams value.

S.archetype_summary: Ball-hawking, rangy free safety with elite instincts and turnover creation ability. Holmes found a franchise safety in the 3rd round with Kerby Joseph, and Branch was a hybrid safety/slot taken in the 2nd round. The archetype is a playmaking centerfielder who can patrol the deep middle and create turnovers, supplemented by a versatile hybrid who can play safety, slot, and box.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Range and ball-tracking ability matter most. Joseph's instincts and ball skills were the draw despite not being a top combine performer. Branch's elite tackling (2% missed tackle rate) stood out.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff. Wants safeties who play fast on film with good range and closing speed.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Split-zone safety concepts. Joseph plays single-high free safety; Branch plays hybrid nickel/safety/slot. The system demands a ball-hawking FS and a versatile hybrid.
most_valued_traits: Ball-hawking instincts and turnover creation — Joseph led the NFL with 9 INTs in 2024
Versatility to play multiple roles in the secondary
Elite tackling and run support (Branch)
Football IQ and recognition skills
Coverage range and closing ability
least_important_traits: Box safety/run-stuffing as a primary skill — Holmes values coverage and ball skills first
Elite combine measurables — both Joseph and Branch were mid-round picks based on film, not testing
deal_breakers: Inability to play in coverage or track the football
Poor tackling fundamentals
Limited positional flexibility
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Ball-hawk ability. Joseph's three interceptions as a rookie confirmed the profile. Branch's 2% missed tackle rate made him an outlier worth reaching for.

college_production.production_floor: Wants evidence of playmaking — INTs, PBUs, forced turnovers. Joseph was a ball-hawk at Illinois. Branch had dominant coverage numbers at Alabama.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year contributors. Branch played 737 defensive snaps at Alabama.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Joseph from Illinois, Branch from Alabama. Holmes evaluates regardless of conference prestige.

examples: 2022 R3.97 — Kerby Joseph: 6-1, 200, Illinois. Ball-hawking FS. Led NFL with 9 INTs in 2024. Perfect fit for split-zone safety concepts.
2023 R2.45 — Brian Branch: 6-0, 190, Alabama. Hybrid safety/nickel. 2% missed tackle rate (best since PFF started tracking). 539 slot snaps. Versatile defender.
2025 R7.230 — Dan Jackson: 6-0, 200, Georgia. Third-team All-SEC safety. 64 tackles, 2 INTs in final year. Late-round depth add with ball skills.

LB.archetype_summary: High-character, do-it-all linebackers with elite athletic testing, tackling reliability, and leadership. Holmes took Jack Campbell 18th overall despite consensus having him in the second round — the ultimate conviction pick. The LB archetype is a physical, assignment-sound, sideline-to-sideline player who brings energy and leadership. Not scheme-dependent; wants guys who play hard.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short-area quickness is critical. Campbell's 6.74 three-cone and 4.24 short shuttle were #1 among LBs at the Combine. Rodriguez was a converted safety — values speed and range at the position.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Must have sideline-to-sideline range. Campbell and Rodriguez are both fast-flow linebackers.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Reliable tackling, assignment soundness, and leadership
most_valued_traits: Elite football character and leadership — Campbell was a multi-year captain at Iowa; Rodriguez was a two-time captain; Barnes: all-conference LB
Tackling reliability and missed-tackle avoidance
Sideline-to-sideline range and pursuit speed
Positional versatility — Barnes played edge and LB at Purdue; Rodriguez converted from safety
Special teams value
least_important_traits: Pass-rush ability as a primary trait
Elite size — Rodriguez at 5-11, 232 was a 6th-round pick who became a starter
deal_breakers: Poor tackling fundamentals
Lack of leadership or character
Taking plays off or inconsistent effort
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite character + athletic testing combination. Holmes gave Campbell a first-round grade when consensus had him in Round 2. Holmes said 'when I say it was only 14 guys (with first-round grades), Jack Campbell wasn't 14th.'

college_production.production_floor: Campbell: 91.9 PFF grade, led all LBs. Barnes: team-leading tackles at Purdue. Rodriguez: team-leading tackles at Oklahoma State. Wants productive, high-volume tacklers.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers multi-year starters with ascending production. Campbell was Iowa's leading tackler for multiple seasons.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values Big Ten and SEC competition. Campbell from Iowa, Barnes from Purdue — traditional Big Ten physical football programs.

examples: 2023 R1.18 — Jack Campbell: 6-4, 249, Iowa. 91.9 PFF grade (led all LBs). Multi-year captain. #1 in 3-cone (6.74) and short shuttle (4.24) among LBs at Combine. Classic high-floor, high-character Holmes pick.
2021 R4.113 — Derrick Barnes: 6-0, 238, Purdue. Hard-hitting, versatile LB/edge who flies around. All-conference recognition. Former all-state RB showing athleticism.
2022 R6.188 — Malcolm Rodriguez: 5-11, 232, Oklahoma State. Converted safety. 2-time captain. Led all rookies with 12 TFLs. Fiesta Bowl MVP. Week 1 starter as 6th-round pick.

WR.archetype_summary: Two distinct archetypes: (1) Game-breaking speed threat with deep-ball ability and elite play speed (Jameson Williams), and (2) Tough, reliable, high-character route runner with elite work ethic who outproduces his measurables (Amon-Ra St. Brown). Both archetypes must pass the football character test. Holmes values WRs who do the dirty work — blocking, special teams — not just pass-catching. The St. Brown model (4th-round value who became a Pro Bowler) reflects the 'intangibles are the separator' philosophy perfectly.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear minimum. Wide range across picks.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Split between two archetypes: Speed archetype (Williams: elite play speed, deep threat ability) vs. Production archetype (St. Brown: sure hands, route running, contested catch). Holmes doesn't require elite combine testing if film and character are exceptional.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Williams was drafted for game-breaking speed. St. Brown was NOT a speed merchant (4.51 40). TeSlaa valued as a 'big, long, smooth strider.' No single speed threshold.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Football character manifested — the moment Holmes and Campbell were sold on Jameson Williams was watching him make a special teams tackle as a gunner, not his TD catches
most_valued_traits: Football character — work ethic, toughness, willingness to block and play special teams
Playmaking ability and dynamic with ball in hands
Versatility — can play inside and outside, contribute in multiple phases
Hands and catch reliability
Route running and football IQ
least_important_traits: Elite combine speed as a prerequisite (St. Brown: 4.51 40, 4th round pick, became All-Pro)
Prototypical size — drafted WRs from 5-10 to 6-2+
deal_breakers: Lack of effort or unwillingness to block/play special teams
Character concerns or history of loafing
One-dimensional skill set
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite play speed + character combination (Williams: traded up 20 spots). Also, rare football character that makes a player much better than measurables suggest (St. Brown).

college_production.production_floor: St. Brown: 178 catches in 3 years at USC, consistent producer. Williams: 79 catches for 1,572 yards and 15 TDs in breakout Alabama season. TeSlaa: led team in catches at Arkansas. Green: 6-2 deep threat from UNC. Lovett: led Georgia with 59 catches.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values consistent producers and ascending trajectories. St. Brown was consistently productive. Williams had a massive breakout junior year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — St. Brown's 4th-round slide was a system/size concern that Holmes didn't share. Williams' ACL tear caused him to drop from top-10 talent.

examples: 2022 R1.12 — Jameson Williams: 6-2, 189, Alabama. Traded up 20 spots. Elite deep speed, 79 catches, 1,572 yards, 15 TDs. Holmes and Campbell sold when they saw his gunner tackle on special teams.
2021 R4.112 — Amon-Ra St. Brown: 5-11, 197, USC. 4.51 40. Projected 2nd-3rd round, fell to 4th. Sure hands, elite route running, incredible work ethic. Became All-Pro. The quintessential Holmes pick.

2025 R3.70 — Isaac TeSlaa: 6-4, 210, Arkansas. Transferred from Hillsdale (D2) where he was a star. Holmes: 'big, long, smooth strider, hands catcher, can play special teams, can block.' Traded up for him.
2025 R7.244 — Dominic Lovett: 5-10, 185, Georgia. Led team with 59 catches. Slot weapon with versatility.
2023 R7.219 — Antoine Green: 6-2, 207, North Carolina. Deep threat with size. Day 3 speed addition.

TE.archetype_summary: Athletic, physical, after-the-catch weapon who can block and contribute as a receiver. Sam LaPorta is the defining pick — Iowa's all-time leader in TE receptions, an elite YAC threat who forced 20 missed tackles (most among FBS TEs), and a willing blocker. Holmes wants TEs who can do it all: win in the passing game, block in the run game, and embody toughness.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Athleticism and YAC ability matter most. LaPorta was an elite athlete at TE. Mitchell was a developmental TE-F/H-B type.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Must be able to create after the catch. LaPorta's 368 YAC and 20 missed tackles forced were defining traits.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Physical, after-the-catch playmaking combined with willing blocking
most_valued_traits: After-the-catch ability and YAC production
Physicality and willingness to block
Football character and competitive toughness
Hands and catch reliability
Versatility to line up inline, in the slot, and flexed out
least_important_traits: Pure in-line blocking specialist profile (Holmes wants receiving + blocking combo)
Elite deep speed at the position
deal_breakers: Unwilling or unable blocker
Soft player who avoids contact
Character concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite YAC ability + physicality. Holmes shocked the football world by taking LaPorta over Notre Dame's Michael Mayer at pick 34. LaPorta played 'an incredibly physical game, something head coach Dan Campbell covets at every position.'

college_production.production_floor: LaPorta: Iowa's all-time leader in TE receptions (153) and 2nd in receiving yards (1,786). 368 YAC and 20 missed tackles forced. Must show receiving production.
college_production.production_trajectory: Multi-year contributor preferred. LaPorta was a multi-year starter at Iowa.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Iowa's physical, run-first offense made LaPorta's receiving production even more impressive. Holmes values production in tough systems.

examples: 2023 R2.34 — Sam LaPorta: 6-4, 225, Iowa. Iowa's all-time TE receptions leader (153). 368 YAC, 20 missed tackles forced (most among FBS TEs). Set NFL rookie record for TE receptions. 10 TDs as rookie.
2022 R5.177 — James Mitchell: 6-3, 250, Virginia Tech. TE-F/H-B hybrid. Coming off ACL injury. Developmental pick with blocking ability and receiving upside.

RB.archetype_summary: Dynamic, versatile, dual-threat running back who excels as a receiver and can be deployed in multiple formations. Holmes made a bold statement by taking Jahmyr Gibbs 12th overall — a pick widely considered a reach at the time. The archetype is an explosive, shifty, pass-catching back who can line up as a receiver and create mismatches. Think a weapon, not just a runner.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosiveness, change-of-direction, and receiving ability. Gibbs was elite in space. Vaki was described as having 'backyard football instincts.' Values dynamic playmaking over straight-line speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Must have game-breaking play speed. Gibbs was one of the most dynamic playmakers in the 2023 class.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility as a receiver and runner — creates mismatches when aligned as a WR or in the slot
most_valued_traits: Receiving ability — Gibbs: 103 catches for 1,215 receiving yards over 3 college seasons; 26.4% of snaps aligned as receiver at Alabama
Explosiveness and elusiveness in space
Versatility to be used in multiple formations and roles
Special teams value (Vaki: 'one of the better special teams players in this entire Draft')
Ball security and durability
least_important_traits: Traditional between-the-tackles power running
Size — willing to draft sub-200 lb backs
deal_breakers: One-dimensional runner who can't catch or pass protect
Fumbling or ball security concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite receiving versatility combined with dynamic playmaking. Holmes reached from 12 for Gibbs despite PFF having him as the 34th overall prospect. The receiving dimension was the differentiator.

college_production.production_floor: Gibbs: 44 receptions at Alabama in final year, 2nd most in school history for a RB. Over 1,000 all-purpose yards. Vaki: versatile RB/S hybrid from Utah.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Gibbs' receiving production in Alabama's offense was a massive signal. Jefferson (2021 R7): small-school star, pure value pick.

examples: 2023 R1.12 — Jahmyr Gibbs: 5-11, 200, Alabama. Elite dual-threat. 44 catches in final year. 26.4% of snaps as receiver. Over 1,000 all-purpose yards as rookie. Holmes' biggest perceived reach — and it paid off.
2024 R4.132 — Sione Vaki: 5-11, 210, Utah. RB/S hybrid with unique versatility. 'Backyard football instincts.' Holmes valued his special teams ability across all four phases.
2021 R7.257 — Jermar Jefferson: 5-10, 206, Oregon State. Day 3 value pick. All-Pac-12. 143 rushing yards per game in 2020.

QB.archetype_summary: Holmes inherited Jared Goff (whom he specifically requested in the Stafford trade) and has not drafted a QB in the first two rounds. His only QB pick was Hendon Hooker in the 3rd round of 2023 — a developmental pick on an injured player. Holmes values toughness, processing ability, and accuracy in his QBs. He explicitly said about Goff that 'toughness is the one trait that goes overlooked.' Holmes appears to believe in building around a competent franchise QB rather than constantly chasing the next elite prospect.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-1 to 6-5 (inferred from Goff at 6-4 and Hooker at 6-4)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Processing speed and accuracy under pressure matter more than any physical test. Goff is not a runner. Hooker had dual-threat ability but was taken as a developmental pick.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard requirement. Values pocket presence and toughness over rushing ability.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Toughness, accuracy, and team fit — not necessarily elite arm talent or mobility
most_valued_traits: Toughness — Holmes specifically cited this as Goff's overlooked trait
Accuracy and ball placement
Leadership and competitive drive
System fit and football intelligence
Deep passing ability — Hooker had 27 completions of 30+ yards (led FBS)
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability
Elite arm strength as a differentiator
deal_breakers: Lack of toughness or competitive fire
Character concerns or lack of leadership
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Not enough data to determine — only one QB drafted, and it was a value pick on an injured player in Round 3.

college_production.production_floor: Hooker: Heisman candidate before ACL tear. 3,135 yards, 27 TDs in 2022. Led FBS in 30+ yard completions.
college_production.production_trajectory: Hooker was a late-blooming transfer who peaked as a senior — Holmes was willing to bet on a player who developed over time.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Holmes valued Hooker as a developmental prospect behind Goff, not an immediate starter.

examples: 2023 R3.68 — Hendon Hooker: 6-4, 218, Tennessee. ACL injury caused massive draft slide. Heisman candidate pre-injury. 3,135 yards, 27 TDs. Holmes: a move for the future, not immediate need.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 QB drafted under Holmes' tenure, and it was a Day 2 value pick on an injured player. Goff (acquired via trade) is the clearest indicator of Holmes' QB preferences. Profile largely inferred from Goff's profile and Hooker's traits.

reach_tolerance.notes: Holmes is one of the most aggressive reachers in the NFL relative to consensus, but his board differs significantly from public boards. Gibbs at 12 (consensus ~34), Campbell at 18 (consensus ~39), Jameson Williams at 12 after trading up 20 spots. In 2023, Holmes revealed the Lions only gave out 14 first-round grades — far fewer than the typical 17-20. His 'reaches' are not actually reaches on his board; they reflect a front office that evaluates very differently from consensus. Holmes has said 'we don't really get anchored on positions' and is willing to take his guy regardless of public perception.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Air Coryell (per PFR). Run-first identity with play-action and deep shots built off dominant offensive line play. Zone-based run concepts. Values versatile OL who can reach-block and move in space. Physical, ground-and-pound mentality. New OC John Morton taking over from Ben Johnson in 2025 — Morton helped install the original offense and promises continuity with his own wrinkles.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: 4-3 base with multiple fronts. Demands versatile defensive linemen who can play multiple techniques. Split-zone safety concepts with a ball-hawking FS and a versatile hybrid safety/nickel. Physical, aggressive defense built around disrupting the line of scrimmage. New DC Kelvin Sheppard taking over from Aaron Glenn in 2025 — Sheppard was already a Lions LB coach, so scheme continuity expected.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Both coordinators changed for 2025 season. OC Ben Johnson left for Chicago Bears HC job; DC Aaron Glenn left for New York Jets HC job. However, both replacements (Morton and Sheppard) are internal promotions who have been with the Lions organization and understand the system. Impact on draft preferences should be minimal. Holmes' non-scheme tendencies (character emphasis, BPA approach, trade behavior) will persist.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderately aggressive. Willing to take perceived reaches for his conviction players (Gibbs at 12, Campbell at 18, trading up 20 spots for Williams). Takes developmental fliers (Manu from Canadian college, Martin from Western Kentucky, Hooker with torn ACL). But also values high-floor players with proven production (Hutchinson, Sewell, Arnold). The net result is a balanced portfolio of safe picks and calculated gambles.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Low recency bias. Holmes explicitly avoids keeping a depth chart in the draft room. In 2022, after the worst defense in the NFL, he drafted 6 defenders — but he says it was board-driven, not need-driven. In 2024, he drafted two CBs with first two picks when it was the biggest need, but Arnold and Rakestraw were also highly rated on his board. Holmes appears to use free agency to address immediate needs, freeing the draft for BPA.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Low sunk cost tendency. Holmes quickly moved on from failed picks. D'Andre Swift was traded for a late-round swap. James Mitchell, James Houston, and Chase Lucas were all let go. Levi Onwuzurike's back injury ended his Lions career and Holmes didn't hesitate to move on. Does not double-down on busted draft investments.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Film-first evaluator who uses measurables as confirmatory data, not eliminators. Drafted Hutchinson despite 32 1/8 inch arms (short for DE), St. Brown despite 4.51 40 (slow for WR), Arnold despite 4.50 40 (underwhelming for CB). Holmes: 'you definitely want to get confirmation on what those measurables are for what you thought you saw on film.' Uses analytics department to study measurable/success correlations but doesn't let testing override film evaluation. However, does value rare athletic profiles for developmental picks (Manu's elite athleticism, Williams' size-speed combo, Campbell's top-of-class agility testing).
psychological_profile.character_description: Highest character emphasis of any GM in the NFL. 'Football character' — defined as passion for the game, mental toughness, leadership, work ethic, and accountability — is Holmes' stated #1 priority and his actual picks confirm it. Nearly every premium pick has been a team captain or multi-year leader. Holmes: 'If these players don't have the intangibles and the passion and the work ethic and the football character, it's not gonna work. That's just the bottom line.' He distinguishes between 'football character' and off-field character, noting young players make mistakes, but football character (effort, passion, accountability) is non-negotiable.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Build around a competent, tough franchise QB rather than constantly chasing elite QB prospects. Holmes specifically requested Goff in the Stafford trade ('Hell, yeah. Absolutely'). Extended Goff long-term. Drafted Hooker as a developmental backup, not a threat to Goff. Will invest in surrounding the QB with elite talent rather than swinging for a QB upgrade. Unlikely to draft a QB early unless Goff's situation fundamentally changes.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Balanced, leaning slightly toward production. Holmes values both productive college players (Hutchinson: 14 sacks; St. Brown: 178 catches; LaPorta: Iowa career records; Campbell: 91.9 PFF grade) AND projection bets (Manu: Canadian college; Martin: raw DT; Hooker: ACL recovery). In tiebreakers, football character is the differentiator, not production alone. Holmes has said 'talent is easy to find' — the intangible piece matters more.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No hard statistical floor — St. Brown had solid but not elite college numbers; Williams' production was elite (1,572 yards). Values film over raw stats.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: No clear minimum — Gibbs had 44 catches (2nd most in Bama RB history). Values receiving production specifically.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: Values demonstrated sack production — Hutchinson (14), Houston (16.5), Hassanein (9.5). Has never taken an EDGE in Rounds 1-2 without significant sack production.
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: No clear production threshold — Martin had 1.5 sacks. Values film, athleticism, and developmental potential at DT more than stats.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: Values turnover creation — Arnold (5 INTs), Branch (dominant coverage grades). Ball skills are the production metric that matters.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: Values tackle volume and efficiency — Campbell and Rodriguez both led their teams in tackles.
production_thresholds_by_position.TE: Values receiving production — LaPorta was Iowa's all-time leader in TE receptions.

college_production_preferences.notes: Holmes evaluates production holistically, not through a single lens. He adjusts for scheme, competition level, role, and usage. A player with modest stats but elite film in a difficult context (SEC, physical system) will be valued differently than a stat-stuffer in a pass-heavy system. The Aaron Donald experience shaped this — Donald's measurables caused others to doubt him, but his film and character were undeniable.

EDGE.threshold_notes: No hard arm length cutoff — Hutchinson drafted at 32 1/8 inches. No hard 40 cutoff. Values film over testing.

OT.threshold_notes: Holmes cited analytics studies on OT arm length/success correlation. 34 inches appears preferred but Sewell was 33.5.

CB.threshold_notes: No hard 40 cutoff — Arnold drafted at 4.50, which was underwhelming. Holmes traded UP for him. Values ball skills and film over timed speed.

DT.threshold_notes: Values size-athleticism combination. Williams at 334 lbs with 4.68 40. McNeill at 320 lbs. Onwuzurike at 290 lbs. Wide range accepted.

WR.threshold_notes: No clear athletic threshold — drafted St. Brown (4.51 40) and Williams (elite speed). Values football character over any single measurable.

LB.threshold_notes: Campbell's #1-among-LBs 3-cone (6.74) and shuttle (4.24) were significant positives.

S.threshold_notes: No clear thresholds. Joseph and Branch evaluated primarily on film and ball skills.

RB.threshold_notes: Values receiving ability and versatility over pure rushing measurables.

TE.threshold_notes: Values athleticism and YAC ability. LaPorta was an elite athlete at TE.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Almost entirely soft preferences. Holmes has repeatedly demonstrated willingness to override measurable concerns when film and character are strong enough. Hutchinson's arm length, Arnold's 40 time, St. Brown's speed — all 'concerns' that Holmes dismissed based on film evaluation and character assessment.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Wide range: 290 (Onwuzurike) to 337 (Martin). No hard size requirement but values presence on the interior.
size_preferences_by_position.LB: Flexible: 232 (Rodriguez) to 249 (Campbell). Will draft undersized LBs with speed and character.
size_preferences_by_position.WR: Flexible: 185 (Lovett) to 210 (TeSlaa). Both small slot types and big-bodied outside receivers.
size_preferences_by_position.OT: Prefers massive tackles: 300+ (Sorsdal) to 352 (Manu). Length and size are valued at OT.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Short-area quickness tests (3-cone, short shuttle) for LBs and EDGE. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness indicators for DTs and OTs. Holmes' analytics department studies measurable/success correlations by position, but testing is confirmatory rather than determinative.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Emphatically film-first. Holmes has built his entire evaluation philosophy around film and character, using measurables as supplementary data. The Aaron Donald experience (undersized DT who Holmes championed based on film) is the origin story. St. Brown (slow 40), Arnold (disappointing 40), Hutchinson (short arms), Manu (small-school Canadian) — all drafted because film and character overrode measurable concerns. Holmes: 'you definitely want to get confirmation on what those measurables are for what you thought you saw on film.'

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Take the highest-rated player on his board with absolute conviction, regardless of position. Willing to trade up for his guy. Does not anchor to positional need. Uses fewer first-round grades than typical (14 in 2023 vs. league average of 17-20). Every first-round pick has been a player Holmes had strong conviction on: Sewell (fell to 7), Hutchinson (2), Williams (traded up to 12), Gibbs (12), Campbell (18), Arnold (traded up to 24), Tyleik Williams (28).
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Extremely active trader in Rounds 2-3. Conducted four trades in the 2023 Day 2 alone. Uses Day 2 to either lock in conviction players (LaPorta at 34, Branch at 45, Rakestraw at 61, Ratledge at 57) or trade down to accumulate picks. This is where scheme fit and roster construction start to factor in more, but BPA still drives the process. Willing to reach for players he has rated higher than consensus.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, high-character special teams contributors, and projection bets. Day 3 is where Holmes has found some of his best value: St. Brown (R4), Barnes (R4), McNeill (R3), Rodriguez (R6), Houston (R6). Consistently targets players with elite football character who fell due to size, speed, injury, or school concerns. Uses Day 3 for positional depth and developmental upside. Has taken developmental OTs (Manu, Sorsdal), raw DTs (Martin), and character-driven specialists (Houston, Rodriguez).
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: No clear pattern of treating comp picks differently. Uses them as regular picks in the draft flow.

EDGE.note: Hutchinson is a star. Paschal derailed by injuries. Houston was productive early but released. Hassanein too early to evaluate.

DT.note: McNeill became a Pro Bowl-level player. Williams (2025) and Wingo developing. Onwuzurike busted due to back injury. Martin didn't develop.

OT.note: Sewell is an elite franchise OT. Manu and Sorsdal are developmental. Sewell alone makes this an elite hit.

WR.note: St. Brown is an All-Pro. Jameson Williams became a 1,000-yard receiver in 2024. TeSlaa and Lovett too early. Green didn't develop.

CB.note: Arnold and Branch are starters. Melifonwu converted to safety, moderate contributor. Lucas released. Rakestraw developing.

S.note: Joseph: NFL INT leader, highest-paid safety in NFL. Branch: Pro Bowler. Elite hit rate.

LB.note: Campbell, Barnes, and Rodriguez all became starters. Barnes earned extension. Elite hit rate.

TE.note: LaPorta set NFL rookie TE reception record. Mitchell didn't develop and was released.

RB.note: Gibbs is a Pro Bowler. Vaki is a hybrid contributor. Jefferson was cut.

QB.note: Hooker has not developed into a starter. Developmental bet that has not yet paid off.

IOL.note: Ratledge, Frazier, and Mahogany are all new or recent picks. Too early to evaluate.

blind_spots: QB evaluation (only one pick, didn't work out). Off-ball EDGE depth beyond Hutchinson has been inconsistent. Day 3 TE evaluation (Mitchell bust).
strengths: Safety evaluation is elite (Joseph, Branch — two Pro Bowlers from Rounds 2-3). Linebacker evaluation is excellent (3 starters from Rounds 1-6). Offensive line evaluation is strong (Sewell is franchise-caliber). WR evaluation has produced two starters including an All-Pro from the 4th round.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate, targeted spender who uses FA to fill specific holes before the draft, freeing the draft for BPA. In 2024, signed Carlton Davis (CB), DJ Reader (DT), and others to address defensive needs, then drafted BPA with conviction. In 2023, signed David Montgomery (RB), Cam Sutton (CB). Holmes explicitly said 'we try to do the best in free agency in terms of plugging holes' so 'you even have more flexibility to not be anchored into a need' in the draft.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a veteran starter is signed at a position, that position becomes less urgent in the draft but isn't completely avoided. After signing Reader at DT, Holmes still drafted Williams (DT) in Round 1 in 2025 because he was the best player on the board. Holmes uses FA to create draft flexibility, not to eliminate positions from consideration.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build-through-the-draft at premium positions (EDGE, DT, OT, CB, S, WR, LB), supplement through FA at shorter-shelf-life positions and to fill immediate holes. Holmes: 'draft-and-develop approach.' Six Pro Bowlers drafted in first four drafts — the most of any team. Holmes is evolving toward a Rams-style willingness to trade picks for proven talent as the roster matures.

notable_patterns: Football character is the non-negotiable filter — nearly every premium pick was a team captain or documented leader
DT is the most frequently targeted position in early rounds (6 DTs in 5 drafts)
Holmes willingly takes perceived reaches when his board diverges from consensus (Gibbs at 12, Campbell at 18, Williams trade-up)
Film-first evaluator who regularly overrides measurable concerns (Hutchinson arms, Arnold 40, St. Brown 40)
Extremely active trader — averaged 2.8 trades per draft, frequently trades up for conviction players
Day 3 has produced elite value: St. Brown (R4→All-Pro), Rodriguez (R6→starter), Houston (R6→productive), McNeill (R3→Pro Bowl level)
Holmes does not keep a depth chart in the draft room to avoid need-driven decisions
6 Pro Bowlers drafted in first 4 drafts (Sewell, St. Brown, Hutchinson, Gibbs, LaPorta, Branch, Joseph) — most of any GM in that span

biggest_reaches.context: Holmes traded down from 6 to 12 and still took Gibbs, who PFF had as the 34th overall prospect. Gibbs became a Pro Bowl RB.
Consensus 2nd-round LB taken at 18. Holmes revealed Campbell had a first-round grade on their board and 'wasn't 14th' among their 14 first-round grades.

biggest_steals.context: Projected 2nd-3rd round pick fell to the 4th. Became All-Pro WR and one of the best receivers in the NFL.
3rd-round safety who led the NFL in interceptions (9) in 2024 and became the highest-paid safety in NFL history.

confidence_notes: 5 drafts of data as Lions GM. Extensive public statements, press conferences, and long-form interviews providing deep insight into evaluation philosophy. PFWA Executive of the Year in both 2023 and 2024. Most prolific and documented scouting tree in the NFL (18 years with Rams). Previous Rams tenure and role in Aaron Donald/Goff/Kupp selections provide additional signal. Profile should be refreshed after 2026 draft.
